verdict.summary: A cash-printing legal monopoly trading at 9× earnings with a 5.7% yield — earnings durable for the next 10-15 years, terminal value the real debate. Position is value + income + tail-risk-tolerant.

pros: Legally-protected effective monopoly: **~80% of India's coal output**, with statutory backing from the Coal Mines (Nationalisation) Act 1973.[^wiki-cil][^wiki-coal-india-industry]
Trading at **9.2× P/E, 5.7% dividend yield, 35% ROCE** — cheap on every multiple, returns multiple of cost of capital.[^screener-cil]
**₹31k Cr FCF in FY26**, balance-sheet net-cash; debt 12% of equity; 47-53% payout sustained for 5 years.[^screener-cil]
Captive customer base: **~80% of dispatches to power sector** under price-controlled Fuel Supply Agreements, protected by India's coal-fired generation share (~70% of grid).[^wiki-energy-india][^cea]
Maharatna PSU with autonomy on capital allocation up to ₹1,000 Cr per investment without Ministry approval.[^wiki-maharatna]
cons: **ROCE compression 107% (FY19) → 35% (FY26)** — base-effect + balance-sheet ballooning + capex spike (FY26 CFI: -₹33,955 Cr).[^screener-cil]
Climate-transition stranded-asset risk: India's net-zero-2070 target + 500 GW non-fossil by 2030 narrows the demand-runway past 2035.[^wiki-climate-india][^wiki-energy-india]
**Government of India owns 63.13%** — pricing decisions politically influenced (notified prices for power); OFS dilution risk at any time.[^screener-cil][^wiki-cil]
Op-margin volatility: **FY26 OPM 24% vs FY24 33%** — a 900bps compression in one year suggests cost-discipline pressure.[^screener-cil]
Capex creep: borrowings 34× since FY15 (₹408 Cr → ₹14,072 Cr); diversification spend (renewables, critical minerals) yet to prove ROIC.
executiveSummary.thesisOneLiner: **Cheap monopoly cash machine** with a clear 10-15-year runway. Buy for income + value; **size to the climate-transition tail risk**.

financials.title: Cash machine at a discount
financials.intro: Coal India operates as a regulated commodity monopoly — pricing partially controlled, volume locked-in via long-term FSAs. Despite a 5-year sales CAGR of only ~12%, FCF generation has nearly doubled. Capital return discipline (47–53% payout) is the real story here.[^screener-cil]

sections.label: Business
sections.title: Section 1 — Do I truly understand it?

questions.title: What does the company actually do?
questions.body: Coal India digs coal out of the ground in India and sells it — mostly to state-owned power plants — at prices that are partly set by the government. It's the **world's largest coal producer by output**, accounts for **~80% of India's coal production**, and operates through eight subsidiaries (ECL, BCCL, CCL, NCL, WCL, SECL, MCL, NEC) plus a planning arm (CMPDI).[^wiki-cil][^wiki-coal-india-industry] The business model: extract from open-cast mines (most volume) and underground mines, transport to power plants via railway, sell at notified prices to power sector under long-term Fuel Supply Agreements (FSAs), and sell residual volumes to non-power sectors (steel, cement, fertilizer) at e-auction prices.
questions.answer: A regulated commodity monopoly — government-owned, price-controlled, demand-locked to India's coal-fired power grid.

questions.title: How do they make money, and from whom?
questions.body: Revenue ~₹1.68 lakh Cr (FY26, ~$20B), 80%+ from coal sales to power-sector utilities under FSAs, balance from e-auctions (premium pricing) and non-power industrial buyers.[^coal-india-fin][^coal-india-pr][^screener-cil] Two structural margin levers: (1) **notified-price differential** — FSA notified prices are well below e-auction spot, so the mix between regulated vs market sales determines margin; (2) **stripping ratio** — open-cast mines have steadily rising overburden:coal ratios, pushing extraction cost up.
questions.answer: Sell government-subsidized coal to government-owned power plants on multi-year FSAs (the cash cow), plus higher-margin spot/e-auction for non-power buyers.

questions.title: Am I pretending to understand it, or do I really?
questions.body: I understand the **economics**: revenue ≈ volume × realized price; the volume is structurally limited by railway logistics + permits; the realized price is a blend of regulated FSA and market e-auction. I understand the **demand driver**: 70% of India's electricity is still coal-fired and the absolute coal demand is still growing despite renewables additions, because base load needs coal until storage gets cheap.[^wiki-energy-india][^cea] What I'm **less confident on** is the inflection year for demand-peak (2032? 2038? 2045?) and the political will to keep notified prices at sub-market levels indefinitely.
questions.answer: Yes on unit economics and near-term demand. **No on terminal year** — that's where I assign a wider error band.

questions.title: Would I be happy owning this for 10 years if the market shut tomorrow?
questions.body:
For the next 10 years (~2026-2036): high confidence. Indian coal demand for power will be **flat-to-mildly-rising** as renewables can only displace marginal supply, not base load.[^wiki-energy-india] FCF generation should sustain ₹20-30k Cr/year. Dividends sustained at ₹20-22/share. Total return = dividends (5-7% yield) + buybacks/modest re-rating = mid-teens IRR.

The 10-year compounding case is straightforward: **buy at 9× P/E with a 5.7% cash yield, get 5-7% annual cash + 2-3% growth + multiple-stability = ~10-13% total**. Better than a Bharat Bond, with optionality from diversification (solar, critical minerals).

What would make me unhappy at year 10? If demand truly collapses 2032-2035 and management cannot deploy capital ex-coal — both real but not base-case.
questions.answer: **Yes for income**; tolerable for total return; not for capital appreciation alone.

questions.title: What could kill this business?
questions.body:
Three real threats, in order of credibility:

1. **Climate transition acceleration** — if India's renewables capex ramp continues at 35-40 GW/year and battery storage drops to ₹2-3/kWh stored, coal-fired plant load factor falls below 50% by 2032, FSA off-take volumes contract.[^wiki-climate-india]
2. **Notified-price politicization** — if state governments squeeze CIL on power-sector prices to subsidize discoms, OPM contracts toward 15-18%.[^wiki-moc][^moc]
3. **Private-mine disruption** — the 2020 commercial coal mining auctions opened the door to private players (Adani, JSW, Vedanta).[^wiki-coal-india-industry] Currently they're <5% of output, but the cap is gone. By 2032 they could be 20-25%.

None are extinction-level. All are margin/multiple-compression risks. The business is **kill-resistant for ~15 years; kill-vulnerable past 2040**.
questions.answer: Climate transition + commercial-mining competition + price politicization. None acute; all real over a 15-year window.

sections.label: Management
sections.title: Section 2 — Can I trust these people with my capital?

questions.title: Would I trust them with my family's money?
questions.body:
Chairman & MD is appointed by the Government of India through the Public Enterprises Selection Board (PESB), not by the board.[^coal-india-board][^wiki-cil] This is the central issue: **the people running CIL are competent technocrats with no skin in the game and limited tenure** (typically 3-5 years before retirement age). Capital allocation is bounded by Ministry approvals.

Trustworthiness on the *operational* side: high — CIL's mine-operation track record is solid, safety statistics steady, production hit ~774 MT in FY24 and is on the **1 BT (1 billion tonne) trajectory for FY26-27**.

Trustworthiness on the *strategic* side: limited — the company has historically deployed cash sub-optimally (large dividends 95-146% in FY15-18 wiped reserves; FY26 capex of ₹33,955 Cr shows shift to investment but ROIC TBD).[^screener-cil]
questions.answer: Operationally yes; strategically **decisions are not theirs to make** — they execute against Ministry policy.

questions.title: How do they treat minority shareholders when no one is watching?
questions.body:
**Decent, by PSU standards.** Dividend payout 42-53% for FY22-FY26 is consistent and predictable. **No dilutive equity issuance** — share count flat at 616 Cr shares since FY19. **Buybacks limited** (no major program announced in last 3 years), but special dividends used to return excess cash.[^screener-cil]

Minority red flags: (1) GoI did **OFS at ₹225 in 2021** when the price was near 10-year lows — not abusive but suboptimal for minority shareholders; (2) FSA notified prices favor government discoms over CIL margin; (3) corporate governance disclosures are SEBI-compliant but minimal voluntary disclosure on segment economics.

The **2020 special interim dividend of ₹7.50** during the COVID demand crash was conspicuous — government extracting cash when minorities needed reinvestment most.
questions.answer: OK but not exemplary. Treat as a **utility-style coupon stock**, not a partner.

questions.title: Capital allocation track record?
questions.body:
Mixed. Three eras:
- **2010-2018 (Cash-hoarding era):** Dividend payouts 95-146% of earnings (sometimes paying out more than profit, draining reserves from ₹34k Cr → ₹14k Cr).[^screener-cil] Government extracting cash from CIL to subsidize state utilities. Capex inadequate.
- **2019-2023 (Re-investment era):** Capex picked up (CWIP rose ₹9k → ₹17k Cr), production grew 8-10%/yr, OPM expanded 22% → 33%. Disciplined.
- **2024-2026 (Diversification + scaling era):** Massive Cash-from-Investing outflows (-₹33,955 Cr in FY26), entry into solar (CIL Solar JV with NLC), critical minerals (lithium, copper) exploration. Returns TBD.

The **right read**: management's job is to execute the Ministry's policy. When the Ministry asks for cash, they pay it. When it asks for production, they ramp. The capital-allocation skill resides at MoC, not CIL.
questions.answer: Capital allocation is **Ministry-dictated, not management-driven**. Recent diversification capex is large; ROIC outcome is the test.

questions.title: How honest are they in disclosure?
questions.body:
**Honest where SEBI requires; minimal where voluntary.** Annual reports are detailed on production tonnage, mine-wise output, FSA volumes, and reserves estimates.[^coal-india-ar] Disclosure of operational metrics (offtake, dispatch, stripping ratios) is industry-standard. Ratings published by COAL India's investor presentations are consistent with audited figures.

What's **less transparent**: (1) **e-auction realization mix** disclosed in aggregate, not by region; (2) **renewables JV economics** for CIL Solar are not segment-reported; (3) **employee welfare costs** (which are very high — ~80% of expenses) are not broken out by subsidiary in segment notes.

No restatements, no auditor concerns in the latest annual report.
questions.answer: Compliant + clean — but lacks the granularity that lets you reverse-engineer the segment economics.

questions.title: Skin in the game?
questions.body:
**Effectively zero.** The Chairman + senior executives hold negligible personal stakes (vesting structure heavily PSU-payscale, not equity).[^coal-india-board] The Government of India holds 63.13% but the **government is not a long-term shareholder** in the traditional sense — it's a parent that periodically extracts dividends and may dilute via OFS.

The ESOP/RSU culture absent in PSUs is a structural minus for alignment, but it's a **double-edged sword**: no management isn't motivated to chase EPS, also not motivated to chase value-destructive M&A or empire-build.
questions.answer: Zero individual alignment; majority alignment is government-as-parent. Treat as **utility, not founder-led growth.**

sections.label: Price
sections.title: Section 3 — What am I paying, and why now?

questions.title: What am I paying for the business today?
questions.body:
**At ₹462 (May 2026), market cap is ₹2,84,998 Cr (~$34B).**[^screener-cil][^bse-cil][^mc-cil] No material debt — borrowings ₹14,072 Cr offset by treasury investments; effective EV ≈ ₹2.75 lakh Cr.

Multiples:
- **P/E (TTM):** 9.17×
- **EV/FCF (TTM):** ~9× (reported); **8.8× on 5y average FCF**
- **P/B:** 2.4× (book ₹193)
- **Dividend yield:** 5.73%
- **FCF yield:** 10.9% (TTM), **~7% normalized (5y average)**

For a business doing 28-38% ROE, 35-78% ROCE over a decade, with a captive customer base and statutory protection, this is **cheap on every multiple**.[^screener-cil] The market discount reflects climate-transition tail risk + governance discount, not operational concerns.
questions.answer: 9× earnings, 7% normalized FCF yield, 5.7% cash dividend yield. **Cheap by every value lens.**

questions.title: What's the business worth conservatively?
questions.body:
Three-scenario DCF (₹ per share):

| Scenario | Assumption | FCF growth | Terminal | NAV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Base** | Stranded by 2045; 10-yr FCF flat, then 5% decline; 10% discount | 0% nominal | Limited | **₹520-580** |
| **Bull** | Demand peaks 2040, gentle decline; diversification covers 30% of EBITDA by 2035 | +3% nominal | Moderate | **₹680-750** |
| **Bear** | Faster transition; demand peaks 2032, declines 4%/yr; OPM compresses to 18% | -1% nominal | Sharp | **₹360-420** |

Weighted (40/30/30): **fair value ~₹530-560**. **Buy below ₹470, load below ₹400, trim above ₹650.**

Cross-check: **a 6% perpetuity** of ₹19,628 Cr (5y avg FCF) = ₹3.27 lakh Cr / 616 Cr shares ≈ **₹530/share**. Validates the DCF.
questions.answer: Fair value **~₹530-560**. Current ₹462 = **~13-17% margin of safety**. Not deep value, but compelling for a yield-plus-growth name.

questions.title: What would I pay for the whole business?
questions.answer: Roughly **today's quote** — a 7% required return on 5-yr-average FCF lands EV near ₹2.5-2.8 lakh Cr. To pay materially below, **wait for ₹400-420**.
questions.body:
A rational private buyer (sovereign wealth, infra fund) would pay for the **5y average FCF of ₹19,628 Cr at a 7-8% required return**, yielding an EV of **₹2.45-2.80 lakh Cr** — close to today's quote.

What I'd pay personally: **a touch below today's price**, recognizing:
- + ROCE 35% on incremental capital
- + 5.7% cash dividend covered 2× by FCF
- + Net cash balance sheet
- + Captive demand for ≥ 10 years
- − Climate transition tail (probability-weighted: 30%)
- − GoI overhang on pricing + OFS
- − No skin-in-game alignment
questions.footnote: Today's quote ≈ what a rational private buyer with a 10-year horizon would pay. Add a **MOS for the stranded-asset tail** by waiting for ₹400-420 to back up the truck.

questions.title: At what price would I sell?
questions.answer: **Trim 50% above ₹650**, exit 75% on fundamental break (3 quarters of FSA-volume decline or OPM < 18%), full exit on capital-allocation foul-up.

triggers.title: Price trigger
triggers.body: > **₹650** (~13× P/E, ~5× normalized FCF yield drops below 3%). Trim 50%.

triggers.title: Fundamental trigger
triggers.body: Three consecutive quarters of FSA volume decline OR OPM falls below 18% → demand-peak is here → exit 75%.

triggers.title: Capital allocation trigger
triggers.body: M&A above ₹15k Cr in non-core (anything outside coal, solar, critical minerals) OR dividend cut > 30% with no buyback offset → exit.

triggers.title: Regulatory trigger
triggers.body: Punitive change to notified pricing (-10% in real terms) OR > 30% production share opened to private competition → trim 50%.

questions.title: If it drops 40% next month — buy or panic?
questions.answer: **Buy.** At ₹277, EV/FCF compresses to ~5.5× and yield to 9.5% — an archetypal asymmetric setup unless one of four red flags triggers (notified-price cut, OFS overhang, RE cost inflection, or board exodus).
questions.body: ₹277 ≈ market cap ₹1.71 lakh Cr, EV/FCF ~5.5× on TTM, ~8.7× on 5y avg, dividend yield 9.5%. **Pre-commitment: buy aggressively** unless one of:
ordered: Coal Ministry mandates a > 15% real cut to notified prices (margin permanently compressed)
GoI announces a > 10% OFS or strategic sale below ₹300 (supply overhang)
Renewable + storage cost curve inflects (RE LCOE < ₹2/kWh with 6-hour storage at < ₹1.5/kWh)
CMD or two of three senior independent directors resign together
questions.footnote: **Buy at ₹400, load at ₹350, back-up-the-truck at ₹280.** A cyclical commodity monopoly trading at a single-digit FCF yield is the **value-investor's archetypal asymmetric setup** — provided the long-term thesis holds.

scorecard.summary: Position sizing: **3-4% at today's quote**, scale to **6-7% on ₹400 sell-off**. Conviction: High on near-term cash; Medium on management strategic decisions; Low on terminal value past 2040. **Value + income + tail-risk tolerance** required.

sources.title: Coal India Limited — Annual Reports landing page
sources.note: Source for production tonnage, FSA volumes, mine-wise output, reserves estimates, and audited segment data. Most recent: FY25 annual report; FY26 results scheduled for May-June 2026.

sources.title: Coal India Limited — Quarterly & Annual Financial Statements
sources.note: Source for quarterly P&L, cash flow, balance sheet. Q4 FY26 (Mar 2026) results filed and audited.

sources.title: Coal India Limited — Press Releases & Earnings Announcements
sources.note: Source for production updates, dividend declarations, FSA renewals, capex announcements, and renewable JV news.

sources.title: Coal India Limited — Board of Directors
sources.note: Current Chairman & MD, functional directors, independent directors, government nominees. CMD is appointed by Public Enterprises Selection Board (PESB).

sources.title: Coal India — Wikipedia
sources.note: Corporate history, ownership structure (GoI 63.13%), subsidiary list (ECL, BCCL, CCL, NCL, WCL, SECL, MCL, NEC, CMPDI), Maharatna status. Primary sources cited in Wikipedia article.

sources.title: Coal mining in India — Wikipedia
sources.note: Industry context — CIL's ~80% share, 2020 commercial-mining liberalization, private-sector entrants (Adani, JSW, Vedanta), national production trajectory toward 1.5 BT by FY30.

sources.title: Ministry of Coal, Government of India — Wikipedia
sources.note: Parent ministry; sets notified prices for power-sector FSA coal. CIL CMD is appointed by PESB under this ministry's purview.

sources.title: Ministry of Coal — official Government of India site
sources.publisher: Government of India · Ministry of Coal
sources.note: Authoritative source for pricing notifications, FSA frameworks, commercial mining auctions, and policy circulars affecting CIL.

sources.title: Coal India Ltd — BSE listing (533278)
sources.note: Authoritative price + corporate-action history. CIL is dual-listed on BSE (533278) and NSE (COALINDIA).

sources.title: Coal India — Consolidated financials & ratios
sources.note: Source for 12-year P&L, balance sheet, cash flow data; quarterly trends; shareholding pattern; ROCE/ROE history. Cross-references audited annual reports.

sources.title: Coal India Ltd — Moneycontrol quote and filings
sources.note: Real-time price, 52-week range (₹369-491 as of May 2026), sell-side consensus, and broker target prices.

sources.title: Maharatna status — Wikipedia
sources.note: Maharatna PSUs (highest CPSE category) enjoy investment autonomy up to ₹1,000 Cr per project without Ministry approval, and 15% of net worth as cumulative cap.

sources.title: Climate change in India — Wikipedia
sources.note: Context for net-zero-2070 target, 500 GW non-fossil 2030 commitment, and the demand-runway implications for thermal coal.

sources.title: Energy policy of India — Wikipedia
sources.note: Generation mix (coal ~70% of electricity supply), national electricity plan trajectory, and the structural demand floor for CIL's volume.

sources.title: Central Electricity Authority — Dashboard & monthly fuel reports
sources.publisher: CEA · Ministry of Power, Government of India
sources.note: Authoritative monthly data on coal-fired generation share, plant load factors, and fuel stock at thermal stations. Validates the FSA volume durability thesis.
